data.title: EU Seeks Feedback on Google's Remedies Following Ad Tech Fine
data.description:
By Edith Hancock

The European Commission is seeking feedback on remedies Alphabet's Google submitted to its competition division after the company was fined 2.95 billion euros ($3.44 billion) over alleged antitrust abuses linked to its advertising technology business.

Google submitted remedies to the commission last month after the watchdog said the company had abused its dominance in advertising technology. The tech giant stopped short of offering to sell parts of the business, a move the regulator had previously indicated it felt was the only way to resolve the issues it identified.

"In order to carry out the assessment, the Commission is gathering further information and feedback from around 200 third parties and relevant stakeholders, based on a non-confidential version of Google's proposed compliance plan," the watchdog said in a statement Thursday.

As part of its remedy package, Google offered to give publishers an option to set varied minimum prices for different bidders when using Google's Ad Manager platform and improve interoperability between its tools and products from other businesses.

The company said when it submitted the remedies that its offer fully addresses the commission's decision without what it sees as a disruptive break-up.

Google has racked up billions in fines from the commission over the past decade for alleged infringements related to widely-used services such as its search engine and Android operating system.

The ad tech fine--issued in September--is the EU's send-largest antitrust penalty ever. The commission said at the time that Google holds an inherent conflict of interest in the ad tech space due to its prominence on both the buy- and sell-side of the sector.

Write to Edith Hancock at edith.hancock@wsj.com

data.title: Bond Yields Rise in the U.S. and Japan — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

Stocks wavered between small gains and losses Thursday, while global bond yields ticked higher with notable rises in benchmark borrowing costs for Japan and the U.S.

The 10-year government-bond yield in Japan rose to 1.941%, the highest since 2007. On Thursday, Bank of Japan Gov. Kazuo Ueda suggested the bank isn't sure how many more interest-rate hikes might be required.

In the U.S., the 10-year Treasury yield increased to about 4.107%. New data showed an unexpected drop in weekly jobless claims, which hit a three-year low during Thanksgiving week. A separate report from Challenger, Gray & Christmas showed its monthly tally of planned corporate layoffs falling back after an October spike, though year-to-date job cuts are at the highest since 2020.

The recent shutdown stopped government statisticians from compiling the flagship nonfarm payrolls report for October, while the November edition has been delayed until after the Federal Reserve's Dec. 10 rate decision. The U.S. central bank is widely expected to cut its short-term target rate.

The S&P 500 was close to flat in midday trading. Shares in Dollar General gained, after the low-price retailer issued earnings and lifted its forecast for full-year sales growth. Kroger stock fell after the supermarket chain swung to a loss. Hewlett Packard Enterprise is among companies slated to issue results after the close.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Export Projections Look to Be Main WASDE Focus — Market Talk
data.description: Next week's WASDE report from the USDA isn't expected to make many supply-side changes to U.S. row crops, says Matt Zeller of StoneX in a note. "Wild discrepancies in the current corn and soybean export paces will be the key focus of the trade for Tuesday's 2025 edition," Zeller says. The story is a bifurcated one for corn and soybeans. "Will the government further cut their soybean export estimate with China slow to buy thus far, and/or further increase already-aggressive corn exports with that campaign off to a record hot start?" CBOT grain futures are higher, with corn up 0.6%, soybeans up 0.5%, and wheat up 0.3%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Fitch Affirms Groupe BPCE at 'A'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Paris-04 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed Groupe BPCE's (GBPCE) Long-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'A' with a Stable Outlook and Viability Rating (VR) at 'a'.

Fitch has also affirmed the Long-Term IDRs of GBPCE's main entities, BPCE SA, the Banques Populaires (BPs), the Caisses d'Epargne et de Prévoyance (CEs), Natixis S.A., Crédit Foncier de France S.A. (CFF) and Banque Palatine S.A. at 'A/Stable', and Banque du Leman SA (BDL) and Banque Internationale de Commerce - BRED (Suisse) SA (BBS) at 'A-/Stable'.

A full list of rating actions is below.

Key Rating Drivers

Leading Retail Franchise, Strong Capitalisation: GBPCE's ratings reflect a strong franchise in France and fairly diversified business model, strong capitalisation, adequate liquidity and a focus on generally low-risk businesses. The ratings also consider weaker profitability than peers and adequate asset quality.

Sovereign Rating Constrains OE: The downgrade of France's sovereign rating led to a downward revision of French banks' operating environment (OE) score to 'a+' from 'aa-', as the score is capped by the sovereign rating. The OE score continues to reflect a strong economic environment and France's robust credit fundamentals. France's ratings are weighed by high debt, a weak fiscal record and political uncertainty, but benefit from a large, diversified, high-income economy. Fitch expects this will continue to support the French banking system's sound performance and stability through economic cycles, while economic growth and unemployment remain broadly stable.

Cooperative Structure: GBPCE is a cooperative banking group bound by an effective mutual-support mechanism comprising its 14 BPs, 15 CEs, its central body BPCE SA and affiliates, including Natixis, CFF and Banque Palatine. The group publishes consolidated accounts, and affiliated entities share a common strategy and risk-management functions. Consequently, all these entities have the same IDRs.

Strong, Diversified Business Profile: GBPCE generates most of its revenue in France, where it has the second-largest retail- and commercial-banking franchise. The group has some diversification in corporate and investment banking and in asset management through Natixis. The transition to higher interest rates has put pressure on GBPCE's performance due to its domestic focused business model. The group has announced several acquisitions, which we expect will support diversification away from domestic retail banking, and should increase the stability and the profitability of its business profile.

Conservative Risk Profile: GBPCE's conservative risk profile is underpinned by the group's cooperative nature and its large portfolio of low-risk home loans in France. The group has reduced its tolerance for traded market risks, which we now view as consistent with its cooperative peers. However, the group's interest-rate risk management proved less effective than that of some peers following the rapid change in interest rates, which led to a material drop of net interest income in 2023-2024.

Adequate Asset Quality: The group's impaired loans ratio (end-June 2025: 3.1%) is higher than most similarly rated European banks. We expect the domestic economic environment to further weigh on the group's asset quality metrics in 2025-2026, and the impaired loans ratio to only slightly decrease from its current level by end-2027. We believe that downside risks to the quality of loans to professionals and potentially more vulnerable SME and corporate portfolios are mitigated by GBPCE's high share of low-risk housing and public sector loans, and adequate coverage by loan loss allowances (58% at end-June 2025).

Weaker Profitability than Peers: GBPCE's operating profit/risk-weighted assets (RWAs) ratio has continued to improve from its trough in 2023 (9M25: 1.3%), but remains materially lower than European peers', due to a thinner net interest margin and weaker cost efficiency. We expect profitability to gradually rebound to about 1.6% in 2027, supported by growth outside domestic retail banking, higher interest margins and tighter cost management.

Strong Capitalisation: GBPCE's capitalisation is a rating strength, with one of the largest buffers above requirements among large European banks. We estimate that Novo Banco's acquisition will reduce GBPCE's common equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio by about 120bp (end-September 2025: 16.4%), but we expect it to remain at a satisfactory 15.5%-16% by end-2027. This is supported by GBPCE's gradually improving organic capital generation capabilities, structurally limited pay-outs to cooperative owners and regular issuance of cooperating-member shares.

Diversified, Stable Funding: GBPCE benefits from a large and granular deposit base from its regional banks in France. Its loans/deposits ratio is higher than European peers (about 120% at end-September 2025), as Natixis and CFF, two of its largest subsidiaries, are mostly wholesale funded. GBPCE's reliance on wholesale funding, which is higher than cooperative peers, is mitigated by its well-established access to market funding. The group's liquidity is managed conservatively, with a sound buffer of high-quality liquid assets.

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

GBPCE's ratings could come under pressure if the French economic environment deteriorated more significantly than we expect, for example due to a larger-than-anticipated effect of a more restrictive fiscal policy or structurally higher risks from the already high private sector indebtedness. Rating pressure would arise if the group's impaired loans ratio rises materially and sustainably above 3%.

The ratings are also sensitive to further setbacks in the recovery of the group's profitability. We would likely downgrade GBPCE's ratings if the group's operating profit/RWAs ratio was sustained below 1%, indicating a weaker business profile, and the resulting lower internal capital generation capacity leading to a CET1 ratio declining below 14%.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

Over the medium term, an upgrade of GBPCE's ratings would require a combination of factors, including a stable record of improved earnings, with an operating profit/RWAs ratio comfortably above 2%, demonstrating a stronger, more diversified business profile. An upgrade would also require a tangible improvement in asset quality, evidenced by an impaired loans ratio well below 3%, while maintaining the group's CET1 ratio sustainably around 17%.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: KEY RATING DRIVERS

GBPCE's 'F1' Short-Term IDR is the lower of two options that map to a 'A' Long-Term IDR, reflecting our 'a' assessment of the group's funding and liquidity.

GBPCE's buffers of subordinated and senior non-preferred debt sustainably exceed 10% of its RWAs (about 12.7% at end-June 2025), resulting in the long-term senior preferred debt of GBPCE entities (including Natixis) being rated 'A+', one notch above the group's Long-Term IDR. For the same reason, BPCE SA's senior non-preferred notes are rated in line with the central body's 'A' Long-Term IDR. We rate the short-term senior preferred debt at 'F1', which is the lower of two options that map to a 'A+' long-term senior preferred debt rating, reflecting our 'a' assessment for funding and liquidity.

The Derivative Counterparty Ratings (DCR) of BPCE SA and Natixis are rated at the same level as the issuers' senior preferred debt rating, as derivative counterparties in France have no preferential status over other senior preferred obligations in a resolution.

The long-term deposits of BPCE SA are rated 'A+', one notch above BPCE SA's Long-Term IDR to reflect the protection that would accrue to depositors from GBPCE's sizeable equity and debt buffers in a resolution. We rate BPCE SA's short-term deposits at 'F1', which is the lower of two options that map to a 'A+' long-term deposit rating, reflecting our 'a' assessment for the group's funding and liquidity.

Fitch rates BPCE SA's subordinated Tier 2 debt two notches below GBPCE's VR, which is the baseline notching for loss severity for these instruments.

GBPCE's Government Support Rating (GSR) of 'no support' reflects our view that although external extraordinary sovereign support is possible it cannot be relied upon. Senior creditors can no longer expect to receive full extraordinary support from the government in the event that the bank becomes non-viable. The EU's Bank Recovery and Resolution Directive and the Single Resolution Mechanism for eurozone banks provide a framework for resolving banks that requires senior creditors participating in losses, if necessary, instead of or ahead of a bank receiving sovereign support.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: RATING SENSITIVITIES

GBPCE's Short-Term IDR could be upgraded if we upgrade GBPCE's Long-Term IDR to 'AA-' or if we revise GBPCE's funding and liquidity score up to 'aa-', which is unlikely. The Short-Term IDR could be downgraded if GBPCE's Long-Term IDR was downgraded to 'A-', and its funding and liquidity score was also downgraded to 'a-'.

BPCE SA's DCR, senior non-preferred debt, senior preferred debt and deposit ratings, and Natixis' DCR and senior preferred debt rating are primarily sensitive to changes in GBPCE's IDRs. We would downgrade the DCRs, long- and short-term senior preferred, senior non-preferred debt and deposit ratings by one notch if we no longer expected the combined buffer of subordinated and senior non-preferred debt to sustainably exceed 10% of RWAs.

The subordinated Tier 2 debt rating is primarily sensitive to a change in GBPCE's VR.

An upgrade of the GSR would be contingent on a positive change in the sovereign's propensity to support the bank. In Fitch's view, this is highly unlikely, although not impossible.

SUBSIDIARIES & AFFILIATES: KEY RATING DRIVERS

Natixis Structured Products Limited

The senior debt issued by Natixis Structured Products Limited and guaranteed by Natixis is rated 'A+', in line with the guarantor's senior preferred debt. We expect the guaranteed notes to benefit from the protection provided by GBPCE's buffer of subordinated and senior non-preferred debt. This reflects our view that Natixis is highly likely to honour its commitment as guarantor if required, as the guarantees are unconditional, irrevocable and timely.

Swiss Subsidiaries

The Shareholder Support Ratings (SSRs) and Long- and Short-Term IDRs of Banque du Leman SA (BDL) and Banque Internationale de Commerce - BRED (Suisse) SA (BBS), which are not covered by the group's mutual support mechanism, are based on shareholder support from their ultimate parent, GBPCE.

Their SSRs of 'a-' and their Long-Term IDRs of 'A-' are one notch below that of GBPCE, which reflects our view of a very high probability of support. Our assessment is based on the material reputational risk that a default of these entities would represent for GBPCE, despite the limited strategic importance of these subsidiaries at group's level. BDL and BBS are also highly integrated within the group in terms of management, capital and liquidity. Fitch does not assign a VR to BDL or BBS as they do not have a meaningful standalone franchise that could exist without the ownership of the parent due to high levels of financial and operational integration.

Their Short-Term IDRs of 'F1' are the higher of the two possible Short-Term IDRs mapping to 'A-' Long-Term IDRs and are in line with GBPCE's. This is because we view the ultimate parent's propensity to support as more certain in the near term, and because we have not identified any potential impediment to the prompt flow of funds to BDL or BBS from GBPCE.

BPCE International et Outre Mer

BPCE International et Outre Mer's (BIOM) IDRs are aligned with its ultimate parent's, GBPCE. This is because we view BIOM's failure risk as substantially the same as GBPCE's due to BIOM's inclusion in the group's mutual support mechanism. BIOM has been in run-off for several years. However, this does not affect our assessment of the entity, as we expect it to remain included in the group's mutual support mechanism. We expect BIOM to continue to operate for several more years, although the scope of the entities it manages may change during this period.

Leasing Subsidiaries

The ratings of the negotiable European commercial paper (NEU CP) programmes of BPCE Factor S.A., BPCE Lease S.A., BPCE Lease Immo S.A., BPCE Bail S.A. and Cicobail S.A., guaranteed by BPCE SA are rated 'F1' and aligned with the guarantor's short-term senior preferred debt. This reflects Fitch's view that BPCE SA is highly likely to honour its commitment as guarantor if required, as the guarantees are unconditional, irrevocable and timely. The issuing entities are part of GBPCE's leasing and factoring activities.

SUBSIDIARIES AND AFFILIATES: RATING SENSITIVITIES

The rating of the senior debt issued by Natixis Structured Products Limited, and guaranteed by Natixis, is primarily sensitive to a change in Natixis's IDRs or to changes in the terms of the guarantee.

BDL's and BBS's ratings are sensitive to a change in GBPCE's IDRs and changes in their roles in, and integration with, GBPCE.

BIOM's ratings are sensitive to a change in GBPCE's IDRs. BIOM's Long-Term IDR could also be downgraded if BIOM was no longer included in the group's mutual support mechanism, which we do not expect.

The programme ratings of BPCE Factor S.A., BPCE Lease S.A., BPCE Lease Immo S.A., BPCE Bail S.A. and Cicobail S.A., guaranteed by BPCE SA, are primarily sensitive to a change in BPCE SA's Short-Term IDR or to changes in the terms of the guarantees.

VR ADJUSTMENTS

The operating environment score of 'a+' is below the 'aa' category implied score due to the following adjustment reason: sovereign rating (negative).

The asset quality score of 'a-' is above the 'bbb' category implied score due to the following adjustment reason: concentrations (positive).

The funding and liquidity score of 'a+' is above the 'bbb' category implied score due to the following adjustment reason: deposit structure (positive).

Sources of Information

The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. Public Ratings with Credit Linkage to other ratings

The rating of Natixis Structured Products Limited's senior preferred debt is linked to Natixis's Long-Term IDR, as Natixis guarantees the notes.

The ratings of BDL, BBS and BIOM are linked to the ratings of their ultimate parent, GBCPE.

The ratings of the NEU CP programmes of BPCE Factor S.A., BPCE Lease S.A., BPCE Lease Immo S.A., BPCE Bail S.A. and Cicobail S.A. are linked to the ratings of their guarantor, BPCE SA.

ESG Considerations

Fitch does not provide ESG relevance scores for Groupe BPCE.

In cases where Fitch does not provide ESG relevance scores in connection with the credit rating of a transaction, programme, instrument or issuer, Fitch will disclose any ESG factor that is a key rating driver in the key rating drivers section of the relevant rating action commentary. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit https://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products

data.title: Serra Verde cuts short China offtake deals, approached by Western firms
data.description:
Key points:
  • Serra Verde renegotiates 10-year Chinese deals, to now conclude end-2026
  • Serra Verde's mine rich in heavy rare earths, unlike many Western deposits
  • Price floors by Western governments seen crucial for rare earths sector growth

By Eric Onstad

Brazilian rare earths miner Serra Verde has slashed the contract periods of its Chinese processing deals, opening up the potential to supply Western companies when their separation capacity becomes available in coming years, its CEO said.

The West has been racing to develop alternative sources of rare earths, vital for defence, electronics, electric vehicles and wind turbines, since China controls 90% of processed global supply.

When Serra Verde's mine was being developed, it agreed 10-year offtake deals with Chinese companies to buy its concentrate for processing since no other options were available.

The Serra Verde mine is rich in heavy rare earths, unlike many other Western deposits, but only now are plants gearing up in the West to process them.

"In a couple of years we'll have some options to separate the heavies outside of China," CEO Thras Moraitis told Reuters.

Privately-held Serra Verde renegotiated the Chinese deals and now they conclude at the end of next year, giving the company multiple options to diversify its customer base, Moraitis said.

"The Chinese, the Americans, the Japanese, the Europeans, the Canadians all have approached us, given that we are the only supplier of heavy rare earths, at least for the foreseeable future."

Forecast shortages of heavy rare earths dysprosium and terbium could be a stumbling block in the West's drive to create domestic supply chains of rare earths and permanent magnets.

PRICE FLOOR ESSENTIAL

Moraitis, formerly an executive of Xstrata, which was acquired by commodity group Glencore, said a price floor guaranteed by governments was key for the development of the rare earths sector outside of China.

The U.S. provided a guaranteed minimum price to rare earths group MP Materials in July as part of a multibillion-dollar investment by the Pentagon and sources told Reuters the mechanism would likely be extended to other firms.

Group of Seven members and the European Union are also considering price floors to promote rare earth production.

Serra Verde's Brazilian mine is an ionic clay mine deposit. The standard extraction technique for such deposits in China and Myanmar has involved flushing the deposit with chemicals, which has caused contamination of water supplies and deforestation.

Serra Verde has spent several hundred million dollars building a plant that does not discharge toxic waste.

The company launched commercial production in early 2024, but has been optimising output so it has not yet hit full output, which is expected to be about 6,500 metric tons of total rare earth oxides a year by 2027.

The U.S. Development Finance Corporation approved a $465 million loan earlier this year.

Serra Verde is owned by private equity groups Denham Capital, Energy and Minerals Group and Vision Blue, which is led by the former head of Xstrata, Mick Davis.

data.title: European Stocks Close Higher
data.description:
European stocks closed firmly higher on Thursday, supported by a rebound for major banks and auto manufacturers.

The Eurozone's STOXX 50 and the broader STOXX 600 both gained 0.5%.

Inditex jumped 2.6%, extending its surge from the previous session after the conglomerate posted strong results and confirmed its full-year outlook.

Similarly, Stellantis rose 3.6% to extend this week's rally to over 10% after being upgraded by UBS. Mercedes Benz, BMW, and Volkswagen added over 3% each to set the pace for the broader auto sector amid upgrades from Bank of America, and Santander, BNP Paribas, and BBVA rose around 2% each to lead the rebound for banks.

Conversely, pharmaceutical giants closed lower with Sanofi and Bayer dropping 1.3% and 2.6%, respectively.

Consolidated bets that the Fed will cut rates next week prevailed despite a strong jobless claims print in the US, lending some support for European equities in afternoon trading.

data.title: Basis bids mostly unchanged as farmer sales remain slow
data.description:
Spot basis bids for hard red winter wheat held mostly steady in the U.S. Plains in slow trading on Thursday as farmer selling remained light, grain merchants said.

  • HRW wheat futures on the Chicago Board of Trade edged higher on Thursday after declines in the prior session. The market was capped by ample global supplies.

  • Farmer selling has been limited as cash prices remain below recent highs.

  • The U.S. Department of Agriculture said net U.S. wheat export sales in the week ended October 30 totaled 505,400 metric tons for shipment in the 2025/26 marketing year, in line with trade estimates for 250,000 to 650,000 tons. The total included 148,400 tons in HRW wheat sales.

  • Statistics Canada said the country's all wheat production in 2025 jumped 11.2% to 39.955 million metric tons, above the average trade estimate for 38.49 million tons.

  • K.C. March hard red winter wheat (KWH26) futures last traded 4-1/2 cents higher at $5.34 per bushel.

data.title: Canada PM Carney to Hold 'Brief' Meeting With Trump Friday
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney expects to have a "brief meeting" with President Trump in Washington on Friday while the two leaders attend the World Cup draw at the Kennedy Center, a spokeswoman said.

No further details were immediately available. A meeting would be a first after Trump terminated trade talks between the U.S. and Canada in October, with Ottawa seeking relief on hefty tariffs on certain key sectors such as steel and aluminum.

Carney's spokeswoman added the Canadian leader would also meet Mexico's President Claudia Sheinbaum during the FIFA event.

data.title: Bahamas plans tougher smuggling laws as US presses migration issue
data.description:
Key points:
  • Bahamas proposes harsher penalties for human smugglers
  • Penalties include fines up to $300,000 and 15 years in prison
  • Bahamas aims to dismantle smuggling routes with US cooperation

By Jasper Ward

The Bahamas is proposing to impose harsher penalties on human smugglers, a move that officials say they hope will help dismantle criminal organizations that use the Caribbean nation as a staging point to smuggle migrants into the United States.

"The Bahamas will not be a stepping stone for illegal migration into the United States," Bahamian Prime Minister Philip Davis told Reuters on Thursday. "We are tightening our laws, stepping up joint operations with the U.S. government and the Trump administration, and dismantling the smuggling routes that run through our islands."

Under proposed legislation, anyone convicted of endangering the life of a migrant, smuggling a pregnant migrant, or smuggling a group of more than 10 migrants would face fines of up to $300,000 and 15 years in prison.

Anyone convicted of arranging the illegal landing of a migrant into another country through the Bahamas would face lesser penalties of up to $100,000 in fines and seven years in prison.

Existing Bahamian law does not address human smuggling, although it does include penalties for unlawfully entering the country and living without proper documentation.

The U.S. State Department did not immediately respond to the move.

Located less than 60 miles (97 km) from Florida, the Bahamas is often considered to be the United States' third border, given the proximity between the countries. As a result, it frequently serves as a transit route for migrants seeking to reach the United States.

Addressing illegal immigration is a top priority of U.S. President Donald Trump's administration. The issue came up when Secretary of State Marco Rubio met with Caribbean leaders, including Davis, in May, according to a State Department readout of the meeting.

Last month, the State Department instructed U.S. embassies abroad to report on the public safety impacts of mass migration, saying it "poses an existential threat to Western civilization."

data.title: US Senate Democrats seek vote on three-year extension of healthcare subsidies
data.description:
Key points:
  • Democrats demand vote on ACA subsidy extension next week
  • Republicans seek policy reforms like income caps, Hyde Amendment restrictions
  • House Speaker Johnson non-committal on Senate healthcare vote
  • Senate Democrats need 13 Republican votes to overcome filibuster on ACA extension

By Nolan D. McCaskill and Richard Cowan

U.S. Senate Democrats will demand votes next week on a three-year extension of an enhanced Affordable Care Act subsidy that is set to expire this month for 24 million people, Minority Leader Chuck Schumer of New York announced in a speech to the Senate on Thursday.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune of South Dakota agreed to give Democrats an up-or-down vote on healthcare legislation of their choosing as part of an agreement with eight Democrats who voted with Republicans last month to end a record 43-day government shutdown.

Democrats instigated the shutdown to pressure Republicans into extending the Obamacare subsidies, which expire at the end of this year. While there is some support for an extension, Republicans have called for policy reforms such as income caps and "Hyde Amendment" restrictions, which prohibit the use of federal funds to pay for abortion coverage.

“Senate Democrats will introduce legislation for a clean three-year extension of the current ACA tax credits,” Schumer said. “Republicans have one week to decide where they stand. Vote for this bill and bring healthcare costs down or block this bill and send premiums skyrocketing.”

The Democratic proposal requires 60 votes to stop a filibuster. Schumer said all Senate Democrats would support the measure, meaning 13 Republicans would be needed for it to advance in the Senate.

SPEAKER JOHNSON NON-COMMITTAL, TIMELINE TIGHT

House of Representatives Speaker Mike Johnson of Louisiana has not committed to holding a vote on any healthcare legislation taken up by the Senate, though the chamber's Republican leaders have said their conference is working on a proposal to make healthcare more affordable. They have not said when such a proposal might be ready for consideration.

Schumer's plan tracks with the approach House Democrats are seeking. It is still unclear whether Senate Republicans will propose a bill of their own next week or later in December.

Congress is running against a tight timeline. December 15 is the last day to enroll in or change insurance plans for coverage beginning January 1.

Republican U.S. Senator John Hoeven of North Dakota acknowledged the time crunch, telling reporters on Tuesday that members are "trying to come up with something that works within the confines of the law that we have now so it can take effect right away."

"That's very much a consideration," he added.

data.title: Finland – Asp buys Pori DC site
data.description:
By Rod Morrison

Norwegian data centre developer Asp DC has bought a 300MW–400MW development site in Pori, CompassForge Real Estate I, from CompassForge Ventures. Construction at the scheme in western Finland is due to begin early next year with the first phase totalling 150MW. Asp DC is part of the Asp Eiendom property group led by Magnus Asp. CompassForge Ventures has been developing data centres in Finland with energy management company Ecogrid.energy and contractor NCC. Avance Attorneys advised Asp on the deal and EY-Parthenon and Hannes Snellman advised CompassForge Ventures.

data.title: Absci Rises on Launch of Trial for Hair Loss Treatment
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Absci shares rose after the artificial-intelligence pharmaceutical company said the first volunteers have received doses in its Phase 1/2 trial for a pattern baldness treatment.

The stock rose 17% to $3.85 midday Thursday. Shares are up 41% in the past year.

The company said it had issued the first doses of ABS-201, an antibody treatment engineered using Absci's AI engine that aims to stimulate hair growth for androgenetic alopecia, commonly known as pattern baldness.

Interim data from the trial are expected in the second half of 2026.

Absci said safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetics data from the trial can be used to support Phase 2 clinical development of ABS-201 for endometriosis, an inflammatory disease affecting the uterus. It said it expects to begin a Phase 2 study for endometriosis in the fourth quarter of 2026, with interim data expected later in 2027.

The company said it would host a virtual seminar on Dec. 11 to discuss the development path and market opportunity for ABS-201.
